seo.title: AI Agent Hallucination Detection in DSPy Agents
seo.description: How to detect and fix ai agent hallucination detection in DSPy agents. Framework-specific detection, fixes, and Vex automation.

content.intro: In DSPy, AI agent hallucinations manifest as modules generating factually incorrect or logically inconsistent outputs during inference, often due to over-optimized prompts or flawed chain-of-thought reasoning. These issues arise because DSPy's signature-based programming and teleprompter optimization can produce brittle programs that excel on training data but fail on real-world inputs. Common culprits include assertion violations slipping through unchecked and unexpected output distributions from module compositions.

howItManifests.scenario: Optimized prompts overfitting to training examples
howItManifests.symptom: Module generates correct answers for train set but hallucinates on OOD inputs
howItManifests.impact: High train accuracy (95%+) but 30-50% hallucination rate on production data

howItManifests.scenario: Chain-of-thought reasoning producing plausible but wrong logic
howItManifests.symptom: CoT module outputs confident step-by-step reasoning that contains factual errors
howItManifests.impact: Model appears to 'think correctly' but reaches wrong conclusions 20-40% of time

howItManifests.scenario: Assertion violations not caught at inference time
howItManifests.symptom: BasicChainOfThought assertions fail silently during optimize() but trigger at predict()
howItManifests.impact: 50%+ of predictions violate core factual constraints post-optimization

howItManifests.scenario: Module composition creating unexpected output distributions
howItManifests.symptom: ReAct or multi-hop agents produce verbose, off-topic responses after optimization
howItManifests.impact: Agent completion rates drop from 80% to 30% due to hallucinated tool calls

detection.name: Signature Output Distribution Check
detection.description: Monitor field-level output distributions against expected patterns using DSPy assertions

detection.name: Assertion Violation Monitor
detection.description: Wrap DSPy modules with runtime assertion checks for factual consistency

detection.name: CoT Logic Validation
detection.description: Parse chain-of-thought reasoning for logical consistency violations

fixes.name: BootstrapFewShot with Anti-Hallucination Assertions
fixes.description: Optimize with hallucination-resistant training examples and strict assertions
fixes.code:
import dspy

class AntiHallucinationSignature(dspy.Signature):
    '''Generate factual answer without speculation.'''
    fact: str = dspy.InputField()
    answer: str = dspy.OutputField(
        desc='Concise factual answer, say "Insufficient information" if unsure'
    )

# Add hallucination-resistant examples
trainset = [
    dspy.Example(fact='Paris is the capital of France.', answer='Paris').with_inputs('fact'),
    dspy.example(fact='The moon landing was in 1969.', answer='1969').with_inputs('fact'),
    dspy.example(fact='What is 2+2?', answer='4').with_inputs('fact'),
]

teleprompter = dspy.BootstrapFewShot(metric=dspy.accuracy)
module = dspy.ChainOfThought(AntiHallucinationSignature)
optimized_module = teleprompter.compile(module, trainset=trainset)

fixes.name: Multi-Module Fact Verification
fixes.description: Chain hallucination-prone module with verification module

fixes.name: Labeled Few-Shot with Uncertainty Examples
fixes.description: Include explicit uncertainty examples in optimization dataset
fixes.code:
def create_uncertainty_trainset():
    return [
        dspy.Example(
            question='What is the capital of Wakanda?',
            answer='Insufficient information - Wakanda is fictional.'
        ).with_inputs('question'),
        dspy.Example(
            question='Who won the 2030 World Cup?',
            answer='Cannot answer - event has not occurred.'
        ).with_inputs('question'),
        dspy.Example(
            question='Paris is capital of?',
            answer='France'
        ).with_inputs('question'),
    ]

# Bootstrap with uncertainty-aware examples
uncertainty_set = create_uncertainty_trainset()
module = dspy.Predict('question -> answer')
optimized = dspy.BootstrapFewShot().compile(module, trainset=uncertainty_set[:50])

vexAutomation.heading: Automating AI Agent Hallucination Detection with Vex
vexAutomation.description:
Vex provides automatic hallucination detection by wrapping DSPy module forward() calls with runtime verification. It monitors output distributions, assertion violations, and semantic consistency against golden datasets in real-time. Vex's integration requires only pip install vex-sdk and wrapping your DSPy modules - no code changes needed.

Vex automatically categorizes hallucinations by type (factual, logical, distributional) and provides remediation suggestions including prompt adjustments and module recompilation. For DSPy specifically, Vex detects over-optimization by comparing train vs inference distributions and flags CoT reasoning failures through semantic entailment checks.

The platform generates DSPy-compatible fix code automatically, including uncertainty-aware signatures and verification chains, reducing manual debugging from hours to minutes while maintaining your existing DSPy optimization pipelines.

cta.heading: Detect and Fix DSPy Hallucinations Automatically with Vex
cta.description: Install Vex with pip install vex-sdk and wrap your DSPy modules in 2 lines of code.
